sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: Summer courses at FHL are offered as individual 9-credit ‘half-term’ (summer A or summer B) courses of approximately 5 weeks. Students can only enroll in one class per half term. They can enroll in A and B term, but have to be separately admitted to individual courses. Approved IFE sections complete the six-credit integrative field experience requirement, with the remaining three credits applied to the ‘other marine biology elective’ requirement. sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: MARBIO 488: Marine Biology in the Field (6 credits): Standard, campus-based course offered annually in the fall with a mandatory field component covered in the three weeks prior to the start of fall quarter (exact dates vary by year) at Friday Harbor Labs. The FHL field component consists of short research training modules focused on intertidal ecology, trophic ecology, and oceanography followed by independent research projects conducted in small groups. The on-campus portion focuses on analysis, presentation, and communication of research project results.

2026 MARBIO 488 Info Session Recording
2026 MARBIO 488 Info Session Slides
3 Year Plan - All Gen Eds Done but need Major Requirements
A minimum of 45 credits taken as a matriculated University of Washington student ( a minimum of 45 of your final 60 credits must be taken at the UW )
Academic programs can also include additional unique courses and credits which can be applied to other Marine Biology Major requirements .
Additional elective courses to bring total credits to 25
Additional requirements
All students complete an integrative field experience (research or field course) at Friday Harbor Labs or at a similar site
Amount: Up to $1,500 for 5 – 6 credit research courses (FHL 470) and $3,000 for 15 credit research courses (FHL 472)
Autumn 2026: Students will be required to arrive at FHL on Tuesday, 9/8/2026 and stay at the labs full time through the afternoon of Friday, 9/25/2026.

Autumn Marine Studies (16 credits): Full-term fall quarter program (approximately 10 weeks) where students earn major elective credits in addition to the integrative field experience requirement. Students select from a limited menu of courses to build a minimum full-time (12 or more credits) schedule:

Students select from a limited menu of courses to build a minimum full-time (12 or more credits) schedule:

Spring Marine Studies Program (15-16 credits) : Full-term spring quarter program (approximately 10 weeks) where students earn major elective credits in addition to the integrative field experience requirement. Students select from a limited menu of courses to build a minimum full-time (12 or more credits) schedule:
Students can choose to meet the ‘missing credit’ for the IFE requirement with the FHL 490 seminar or with other marine biology electives taken at any time (Seattle campus or FHL).

Students select from a list of eligible courses which give hands-on experience with changes in the marine environment at a variety of scales (over time and space) and systems (physical and biological). "/>

Students select from a list of eligible courses which give hands-on experience with changes in the marine environment at a variety of scales (over time and space) and systems (physical and biological). ">

Students select from a list of eligible courses which give hands-on experience with changes in the marine environment at a variety of scales (over time and space) and systems (physical and biological). These advanced courses build on the foundational knowledge developed across the three core areas of the major (biodiversity, ecology and ecosystems and organismal processes) and develop skills in fieldwork techniques, data analysis and science communication.

displayCourseCodes: MARBIO 488: Marine Biology in the Field (6 credits): Standard, campus-based course offered annually in the fall with a mandatory field component covered in the three weeks prior to the start of fall quarter (exact dates vary by year) at Friday Harbor Labs. The FHL field component consists of short research training modules focused on intertidal ecology, trophic ecology, and oceanography followed by independent research projects conducted in small groups. The on-campus portion focuses on analysis, presentation, and communication of research project results.

parsedRequirementCourses.courseCode: FHL 432: Marine Invertebrate Zoology (9 credits): Comparative morphology and biology of marine invertebrates with emphasis on field and laboratory studies. Representatives of all major and most minor phyla are collected, observed live, and studied in detail. (Offered every year)
parsedRequirementCourses.title: Marine Invertebrate Zoology
parsedRequirementCourses.sourceHeading: Summer courses at FHL are offered as individual 9-credit ‘half-term’ (summer A or summer B) courses of approximately 5 weeks. Students can only enroll in one class per half term. They can enroll in A and B term, but have to be separately admitted to individual courses. Approved IFE sections complete the six-credit integrative field experience requirement, with the remaining three credits applied to the ‘other marine biology elective’ requirement. Summer offerings vary by year: see the list below for upcoming approved courses

parsedRequirementCourses.courseCode: FHL 468: Advanced Topics in Ecology and Biomechanics (9 credits) : Special topics course listing where courses vary by offered section. Topics can focus on various aspects of ecology and biomechanics of marine organisms, such as experimental ecology, ocean acidification, deep sea biodiversity, or biomechanics.

parsedRequirementCourses.courseCode: MARBIO 488: Marine Biology in the Field (6 credits): Standard, campus-based course offered annually in the fall with a mandatory field component covered in the three weeks prior to the start of fall quarter (exact dates vary by year) at Friday Harbor Labs. The FHL field component consists of short research training modules focused on intertidal ecology, trophic ecology, and oceanography followed by independent research projects conducted in small groups. The on-campus portion focuses on analysis, presentation, and communication of research project results.
